pattern: XORing the two numbers leaves a 1 in every bit position where they differ, so the answer is simply the number of set bits in that XOR.
hints: A bit position contributes to the distance exactly when the two numbers disagree there. Is there a single bitwise operator that produces a 1 precisely at the disagreeing positions?
Compute x XOR y; every set bit of the result marks a position where x and y differ. The problem reduces to counting set bits.
Count the 1 bits of x ^ y, for example by repeatedly clearing the lowest set bit (v &= v - 1) or using a built-in popcount.

edge_inputs.description: the two statement values, differing in two positions
adjacent values differing in a single bit
equal nonzero numbers still have distance zero
zero versus the maximum 31-bit value flips all 31 bits
values differing only in the highest allowed bit

stress.description: random pairs across the full 31-bit range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    return [rng.randint(0, size), rng.randint(0, size)]
stress.note: covers arbitrary bit patterns including high bits
